coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of green fluorescent carbon materials using byproducts of the sulfite-pulping procedure residue for live cell imaging and Ag+ ion determination
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Lina
Yang, Xiaohua
Ding, Haiyang
Li, Shouhai
Li, Mei
Wang, Dan
Xia, Jianling
coredata.dc:description: A simple synthesis strategy was designed and applied to synthesize nitrogen and sulfur co-doped aminated ligninsulfonate/graphene quantum dots (ASL/GQDs) composites using citric acid monohydrate and byproducts of the sulfite-pulping procedure (sodium lignosulfonate). The combination of these two materials improves surface chemical activities and electronic characteristics. As a result，the combination offers excellent photoluminescence properties and sensitivity. The fluorescence intensity of the as-prepared ASL/GQDs composites is more than three times that of the free GQDs. ASL/GQDs based fluorescent probe was applied to sensitively determine Ag+ with a good linearity in a range from 0.005 to 500 μM with a correlation coefficient of 0.99. The method was also used successfully to determine the amount of Ag+ in environmental water samples. Using an MTT assay, the ASL/GQDs have low toxicity and are biocompatible with A549 cells, and may be successfully used to image A549 cells.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lignosulfonate
Fluorescent sensor
Silver ion
Cell imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of the ASL/GQDs composites and GQDs
The selectivity and sensitivity of probe ASL/GQDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The TEM images of (a) ASL, (b) ASL/GQDs.
UV–vis absorption spectra of ASL, GQDs and ASL/GQDs.
The PL excitation (a, c) and emission (b, d) spectra of the GQDs and ASL/GQDs.

PL emission spectra (a) of ASL/GQDs dispersion in the presence of different concentrations of Ag+ (0, 0.005, 0.05, 0.5, 5, 10, 50, 100, 200, 300, 400, and 500 μM). (b) A linear relationship of (F/F0) versus the concentration of Ag+.
Quenching efficiency of the ASL/GQDs solution in the absence and presence of various metal ions ([Mn+] = 50 μM).

Confocal fluorescence images of A549 cells incubated with ASL/GQDs (a, b), ASL/GQDs and Ag+ (50 μM) (c, d). (a) and (c) bright field image; (b) and (d) fluorescence image in the green channel. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Comparison of different fluorescent sensors for Ag+ detection.
Determination results of ASL/GQDs in real samples.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of green fluorescent carbon materials using byproducts of the sulfite-pulping procedure residue for live cell imaging and Ag+ ion determination

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Chemical Industry of Forestry Products, CAF, Key Lab. of Biomass Energy and Material, Jiangsu Province, National Engineering Lab. for Biomass Chemical Utilization, Key and Lab. on Forest Chemical Engineering, SFA, Co-Innovation Center of Efficient Processing and Utilization of Forest Resources, Nanjing Forestry University, Nanjing 210042, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Guangxi Teachers Education University, Nanning 530001, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Materials Science

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A simple synthesis strategy was designed and applied to synthesize nitrogen and sulfur co-doped aminated ligninsulfonate/graphene quantum dots (ASL/GQDs) composites using citric acid monohydrate and byproducts of the sulfite-pulping procedure (sodium lignosulfonate). The combination of these two materials improves surface chemical activities and electronic characteristics. As a result，the combination offers excellent photoluminescence properties and sensitivity. The fluorescence intensity of the as-prepared ASL/GQDs composites is more than three times that of the free GQDs. ASL/GQDs based fluorescent probe was applied to sensitively determine Ag+ with a good linearity in a range from 0.005 to 500 μM with a correlation coefficient of 0.99. The method was also used successfully to determine the amount of Ag+ in environmental water samples. Using an MTT assay, the ASL/GQDs have low toxicity and are biocompatible with A549 cells, and may be successfully used to image A549 cells.

ce:section.ce:para: Silver is an essential heavy transition metal and has been widely used as a key role in the photographic and imaging industry, electronics, and pharmacy over the past decades [1–3]. However, silver ion is one of highly toxic heavy metals. Silver ion inactivates enzymes by reacting with the sulfhydryl groups in the body to cause numerous health problems. The heavy metals are environmentally damaging as well [4–11]. Accordingly, developing a selective and sensitive detection method for trace amounts of Ag+ in in environmental and biological samples is of considerable significance. Up to now, various quantitative methods have been developed for the determination of Ag+, such as potentiometry and electrothermal atomic absorption spectrometry [12,13]. However, these traditional quantitative methods of detection are somewhat costly and laborious [14,15].

The fluorescence strategy is a popular method for detecting metal ions as it offers sensitive luminescence signals, fast analysis, low cost, and sample preservation [16–24]. Many fluorescent sensors (e.g. organic dyes, metal nanoclusters, semiconductor quantum dots, etc.) have been developed to ions detection. However, these fluorescent probes generally suffer from intrinsic defects such as complex synthesis routes or potential toxicity [25–28]. Hence, low-cost alternatives with good biocompatibility are needed. Fluorescent graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have attracted considerable attention for their unique properties such as water-solubility, environmental safety, stable photoluminescence (PL), tunable surface functionalities, and simple synthetic routes [29–32].

Methods of synthesizing GQDs can be divided into two categories, top-down and bottom-up, based on the direction of fabrication [33,34]. The top-down strategies attempt to break off GQDs from bulk materials, while the bottom-up strategies seek to build up GQDs from small molecules using thermal treatment. GQDs synthesized by these methods commonly have relatively low fluorescence, quantum-yield, and fluorescence intensity. Passivation and doping with heteroatoms are popular methods for enhancing the luminescence of GQDs [35]. However, the modification process is often time-consuming and infeasible for large-scale production, further limiting their usefulness. The evolution of new synthesis strategies is required to produce high luminescence GQDs.

Previously, our group reported a one-step, in-situ, lignin-assisted method that produces highly luminescent, uniformly distributed, core/shell structures GQDs composites [36]. Lignin sulfonates are byproducts of the paper industry. Only 10% of these lignin sulfonates can be recycled (as low-value products), resulting in environmental pollution and resource waste. The lignin sulfonate macromolecule contains functional groups (e.g., negatively charged sulfonate, hydroxyl, and phenolic) which produce potential complexation sites for metal ions through cation exchange. We found that the combination of GQDs and lignin sulfonate can offer excellent photoluminescent properties and good selectivity toward Fe3+. However, it is not known whether structural changes in lignin influence fluorescence performance. Furthermore, the relationship between complexation sites and a selectively identified target has not been studied.

The present work reports a facile fabrication of N, S co-doped aminated lignin sulfonate/graphene quantum dot (ASL/GQDs) composite that combines the advantages of GQDs and lignin. The combination offers excellent PL and high selectivity and sensitivity for Ag+ detection. We further demonstrate the usefulness of ASL/GQDs in bioimaging, suggesting potential uses in the biomedical field.

Sodium lignosulfonate were obtained from Aladdin chemistry Co. Ltd. and used as received. Other chemicals used in this work were commercially available. Distilled water was applied for all of the experiments.

Fluorescence spectra were obtained from an LS SS Perkin Elmer spectrometer. Ultraviolet-Visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were characterized by a Shimadzu UV-2550 PC spectrometer. NMR spectra were collected on a Bruker AN-400 spectrometer and reported in ppm (TMS as internal standard). The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra of the product were conducted on a Nicolet Nexus 670 Fourier transform infrared spectroscope. The morphologies of the as-prepared products were observed under a Technai G2 20 S-Twin transmission electron microscopy (TEM). Live cell imaging were measured by using confocal laser scanning microscopy (Nikon A1R).

Aminated sodium lignosulphonate (ASL) was synthesized by Mannich reaction. In a typical synthesis, sodium lignosulfonate (10 g) and distilled water were poured into 50 mL three-necked flask and vigorously stirred to ensure that the sodium lignosulphonate was fully dissolved. Next, the pH was adjusted to a range of 8–13 by NaOH. Diethylenetriamine was then added and the temperature carefully elevated to 70 °C. Formaldehyde was then added dropwise and the mixture kept at 70 °C for 4 h under stirring. After cooled to room temperature, the solution yielded a dark brown precipitate at a pH of 3.0–4.0 by adding 0.1 M HCl. It was collected by filtration, and then washed with distilled water and ethanol several times. After that, brown solid product was obtained by drying in vacuum. The powder was stored in a dry ambient condition for further characterization.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the ASL/GQDs composites and GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: 2 g CA was added into a glass container and heated to 200 °C for 15 min until the reaction system was changed from colorless solid to a yellow liquid. After that the above liquid was added into a mixture of ASL/H2O (0.09 g/40 mL) and neutralized by adding NaOH solution and stirred vigorously for 3 h. For comparison, GQDs were synthesized using the same approach, except without the addition of ASL dispersion.

ASL/GQDs dispersion (0.3 mL), PBS buffer (pH 7.0, 3 mL) and different concentrations of Ag+ or other metal ion (Ca2+, Cd2+, Cu2+, Co2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Zn2+, Al3+, and Fe3+) were added into the cuvette. After addition of an analyte for 10 min at room temperature, the PL spectra were recorded.

The MTT assay was carried out to measure the cytotoxicity of ASL/GQDs in A549 cells. A549 cells were plated on a 96-well plate with 90 μL per well overnight at room temperature. The cells then treated with ASL/GQDs suspensions with different concentrations (20, 40, 60, 80, and 100 μg/mL). After that the cells were further incubated for 24 h, and 20 μL MTT reagent (1 mg/mL) was added to every well. After all cells were further cultured for 4 h, the culture medium was removed and 150 μL DMSO solution was added into every well. The obtaining mixture was shocked in dark for 15 min, and its optical density (OD) was acquired on a microplate reader (Thermo TY10FC, USA).

Human A549 cells were cultured in Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium with 10% fetal bovine serum and 1% penicillin in humidified environment of 5% CO2 at 37 °C. For living cell imaging experiments of ASL/GQDs, cells were incubated with 20 μg/mL of ASL/GQDs composites for 30 min at 37 °C, followed by incubation for 24 h. Finally, the cells were washed.

The water-soluble ASL/GQDs were readily prepared by hydrothermal treatment of CA into ASL solution. ASL was characterized by 1H NMR and FT-IR (Figs. S1, S2). Additionally, morphologies of the as-prepared ASL and ASL/GQDs are shown in Fig. 1 . In the TEM micrograph (Fig. 1a), the ASL agglomerates, while the ASL/GQDs has a typical microsphere structure with a smooth surface (Fig. 1b) in which ASL pucker together in the form of microspheres and GQDs are located around ASL microspheres by shielding the intermolecular electrostatic repulsion of ASL. Besides, the size distribution of ASL/GQDs ranges from 310 to 770 nm. XPS analysis was employed to investigate the surface composition of ASL/GQDs, as shown in Fig. S3. The signal of C 1s (ca. 283 eV), N 1s (ca. 399 eV), and O 1s (ca. 531 eV) are observed in the full survey XPS. The peaks corresponding to C 1s and O 1s are obviously observed, and the C and O contents are 47.80 and 51.69 at.%, respectively. The detailed C 1s spectrum reveals three peaks at 284.2, 285.2, and 288.0 eV, respectively, associated with the CC/CC, CO/CN/CS, and CO(N) groups (Fig. S4). But the peaks of S and N are very small. This means that the ASL/GQDs have many surface oxygen-containing groups, and ASL is not on the surface, further demonstrate that ASL/GQDs displays core/shell morphology.

As a class of renewable resource materials with various active functional groups, derivatives of lignin have drawn much attention. Lignin could combine with GQDs facilely by π-π stacking. The combination of these two materials may offer advantages such as excellent photoluminescence and good sensitivity. Meanwhile, ASL/GQDs solution remains photostability under continuous UV irradiation over 20 min, no obvious reduction in fluorescence intensity was observed.

In this work, a core/shell hybrid based on ASL and GQDs was developed. The UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectroscopy were investigated to study the optical properties of the prepared ASL/GQDs. As shown in Fig. 2 , the prepared ASL/GQDs are water-soluble and exhibit a strong blue luminescence under a UV lamp (362 nm) and a narrow full width, which reflects that the sp2 clusters contained in ASL/GQDs should be uniform in size. However, the GQDs exhibit an absorption band at 358 nm. Furthermore, the UV–vis absorption peak of the ASL/GQDs is higher than that of the free GQDs. This indicates that doping with heteroatoms have greatly affected the photoluminescent properties of GQDs. Moreover, pristine ASL has nearly no absorption above 260 nm, indicating that the pristine ASL solution is non-emissive in the visible region and confirming that the PL originates from the GQDs.

As shown in Fig. 3 , the PL emission peak of the prepared ASL/GQDs centered at 462 nm, when excited at 362 nm, indicates that ASL/GQDs are fluorescent. The excitation peak of ASL/GQDs and GQDs is almost as high as the emission peak of them, which indicates that ASL/GQDs and GQDs have limited non-emitting absorption. However, the ASL/GQDs and GQDs have some differences in fluorescence performance. The PL intensity of ASL/GQDs composites is more than three times that of the GQDs. This is likely due to surface passivation of GQDs through the incorporate of heteroatoms and the acid-base-type interaction between the amine groups of ASL and the carboxylic groups of the GQDs [37,38]. Moreover, ASL microspheres are likely in favor of assembling the incompletely carbonized CA around them by π–π interaction, which may also surface passivate the GQDs [36,39].

The influence of pH on the optical responses of the ASL/GQDs was investigated by changing the pH value ranging from 4 to 12 (Fig. S5). When pH changes from 7 to 12, the fluorescence intensity of the ASL/GQDs has no obvious change. But when the pH value decreases to less than 5, the PL intensity of ASL/GQDs decreases quickly, indicating the ASL/GQDs can be used in both weak acidic and alkaline environments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The selectivity and sensitivity of probe ASL/GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Silver ion is considered to be one of the most toxic heavy metal ions in existence, however, traditional quantitative methods developed for the determination of Ag+ are somewhat limited. We investigated the feasibility of using the prepared ASL/GQDs as a sensor for the detection of Ag+. As shown in Fig. 4a, the PL emission of ASL/GQDs is quenched proportionally with the increasing concentration of Ag+. Meanwhile, from Job's plot analysis, the good linear relationship of (F/F0) versus the concentration of Ag+ is obtained in range of 0.005–500 μM, with a correlation coefficient of 0.99 (Fig. 4b). And the limit of detection (LOD) for Ag+ is 4.70 μM based on a signal-to-noise ratio of 3. As shown in Table 1 , ASL/GQDs showed better linear ranges than others, and it provided better sensitivity in comparison with N,S-CDs [40] and CNC-OMe [43].

Further research demonstrates that the significant fluorescence quenching response of ASL/GQDs was specific for silver ion, as the PL intensity is almost no change by the addition of various ions such as Cd2+, Cu2+, Co2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Ca2+, Ni2+, Zn2+, Al3+, and Fe3+, etc. (Fig. 5 ). These results further confirm the high sensitivity and selectivity of the ASL/GQDs toward Ag+. It has been reported that the d orbital of Ag+ more easily receives electrons in the ASL/GQDs excited state by non-radiative electron transfer than other metal ions. Furthermore, the coordination between sulfonic acid group and Ag+ resulted in fluorescence quenching [44–46]. Here, Ag+ were added into GQDs, diethylenetriamine, and ASL/GQDs, no obvious change were observed for the GQDs or diethylenetriamine, but flocculation appeared in the ASL/GQDs (Fig. S6). Thus, the synergistic interaction results between GQDs and ASL, leading to the PL quenching behavior of ASL/GQDs on Ag+. Indeed, ASL/GQDs solution showed good reversibility when a strong Ag+ chelator, ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA), was added into the ASL/GQDs mixture (Fig. S7). We also examined Ag+ detection in the presence of 100-fold Zn2+, Cu2+ or Ca2+ as interfering ions as to investigate the effectiveness of ASL/GQDs. As shown in Fig. S8, the interfering metal ions just slightly affected the fluorescence intensity of the system.

For a real-world test of ASL/GQDs sensitivity to Ag+, the ASL/GQDs were applied to measure Ag+ spiked in tap water samples. The recovery study of Ag+ measured by ASL/GQDs was listed in Table 2 . The results indicate that the ASL/GQDs system produce a quantitative measure of Ag+ in practical environmental samples.

The ASL/GQDs can be effective in in living system, due to its excellent biocompatibility and strong photoluminescence. To evaluate the cytotoxicity of ASL/GQDs, we preformed the standard MTT assay utilizing A549 cells as the model. As depicted in Fig. S9, the viability of the A549 cells remained greater than 89% even after the addition of ASL/GQD (100 μg/mL) for 24 h, indicating that the ASL/GQDs has the low toxicity and excellent biocompatibility. As illustrated in Fig. 6 , a strong green fluorescence in the cell cytoplasm was observed after the cells were incubated with ASL/GQDs (a, b), but there was significant fluorescence quenching was observed after the cells were treated with ASL/GQDs and Ag+ (50 μM) (c, d). These results suggest that the bright fluorescence ASL/GQDs can act as potential efficient cellular imaging and new fluorescent sensor for intracellular Ag+.

We synthesized N, S co-doped ASL/GQDs by one-step in-situ reaction using citric acid monohydrate and byproducts of the sulfite-pulping procedure residue (ASL). ASL played two important roles in this hybrid composites system. The first was surface passivation of GQDs through the incorporation of nitrogen and sulfur atoms resulted in strong PL. The second was offering functional groups to selectively identify Ag+ ions. This sensing platform offers enriched routes to the surface passivation of nanomaterials with low luminescence and the development of new functional materials for the selective and sensitive detection of a target by a facile and cost-efficient route. The probe has other merits such as biocompatibility, low detection limits, and a proper linear relationship for Ag+. In addition, the cell imaging results suggest that ASL/GQDs is a good imaging agent for bioimaging and high sensitivity toward the Ag+ in biological systems. Overall, this novel composite offers an environmentally friendly fluorescent sensing platform for detection and imaging, which are expected to mass production at relatively low cost.
This study was supported by the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Non-profit Research Institution of CAF (grant number: CAFYBB2017QA016).
There are no conflicts to declare.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.msec.2019.04.014.
